---
title: "Private Estimation - cont"
subtitle: "Denominators, local sensitivity, and private control flow"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: white
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    scrollable: true
    css: slides.css
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| include: false
try:
    ip = get_ipython()
    if "autoreload" not in ip.extension_manager.loaded:
        ip.run_line_magic("load_ext", "autoreload")
        ip.run_line_magic("autoreload", "2")
except Exception:
    pass

try:
    import libdpy
except ImportError:
    %pip install -q "libdpy[notebook] @ git+https://github.com/applied-dp-course/pub_lib.git"
    import libdpy

%matplotlib inline
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| include: false
from libdpy.assignment_specific.private_subgroup_comparisons.lecture_figures import (
    make_global_sensitivity_baseline_figure,
    make_local_sensitivity_vs_support_figure,
    make_noisy_count_sum_release_figure,
    make_oracle_ls_neighbor_bars_figure,
    make_oracle_ls_privacy_failure_figure,
    make_oracle_ls_utility_figure,
    make_oracle_vs_count_sum_audit_figure,
    make_sparse_subgroup_warning_figure,
    make_support_comparison_figure,
    make_support_repair_leaderboard_figure,
    make_subgroup_mechanism_leaderboard_figure,
    make_subgroup_sampling_distribution_figure,
    make_above_threshold_support_search_figure,
)

SEED = 42
```

## From clipping to subgroup analysis

::: {.fragment}
We already controlled the salary range with a public clip interval.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Now **denominators** control accuracy for subgroup comparisons.
:::

## The statistic

::: {.fragment}
Difference of two **normalized** group means on clipped salaries.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Dollar-scale estimates are post-processing: multiply by `(U - L)`.
:::

## Sampling variability before privacy

::: {.fragment}
Ordinary statistical uncertainty for the subgroup difference before adding privacy noise.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_subgroup_sampling_distribution_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Global sensitivity is pessimistic

::: {.fragment}
Tiny groups dominate the worst case: `GS_Δ ≤ 2` on the normalized scale.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_global_sensitivity_baseline_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Local sensitivity explains typical utility

::: {.fragment}
Conservative bound scales like `1/n_A + 1/n_B`.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_local_sensitivity_vs_support_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Temptation: scale noise by local sensitivity

::: {.fragment}
Looks excellent on balanced datasets — **not DP**.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_oracle_ls_utility_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Engineered sparse support

::: {.fragment}
Same 990/10 split as Lecture 5: denominator-fragile even before privacy.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_sparse_subgroup_warning_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Why the tempting mechanism is not private

::: {.fragment}
Neighboring datasets can change the **noise scale** when support drops.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Local sensitivity is an excellent diagnostic, but a bad noise scale.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_oracle_ls_neighbor_bars_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Audit the released estimate

::: {.fragment}
The privacy failure is visible in the output distribution, not only in the internal scale.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_oracle_ls_privacy_failure_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Same witness, valid repair

::: {.fragment}
Noisy count/sum spends budget on denominators instead of adapting the noise scale for free.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_oracle_vs_count_sum_audit_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Valid baseline: release counts and sums

::: {.fragment}
Four Laplace queries + public denominator floor `τ`.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Counts are not free, but sometimes they are the right output.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_noisy_count_sum_release_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## PTR: test support, then release or abstain

::: {.fragment}
The test estimates distance from the bad set.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Release uses a public sensitivity bound once the test certifies enough distance.
:::

## Smooth sensitivity

::: {.fragment}
Nearby datasets matter; noise grows smoothly near sparse regions.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Conceptual comparison — not a formal pure-DP guarantee in this deck.
:::

## Common support comparison

::: {.fragment}
Vary true minimum support `min(n_A, n_B)` — not only the PTR threshold `m`.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_support_comparison_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Sparse vs dense support repairs

::: {.fragment}
Conditional PTR error is only shown where releases are common enough to estimate honestly.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_support_repair_leaderboard_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Fulton mechanism leaderboard

::: {.fragment}
Same Lecture 5 signed-error workflow, now comparing subgroup mechanisms.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_subgroup_mechanism_leaderboard_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Public data gives a menu

::: {.fragment}
Public coarsenings and subgroup definitions are free to propose.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Private validation of support costs privacy budget.
:::

```{python}
fig = make_above_threshold_support_search_figure(seed=SEED)
fig
```

## Summary

::: {.fragment}
After clipping, subgroup analysis is about **denominators and private control flow** — not extreme outliers.
:::

::: {.fragment}
Public coarsenings propose questions; private validation of support costs budget.
:::
